subject: Internal medicine
title: Finger injuries
preambleMarkdown:
# Finger injuries

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Finger injuries is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Finger injuries to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Finger injuries is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Finger injuries depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Finger injuries.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Finger injuries disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Finger injuries should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Finger injuries first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Finger injuries, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Finger injuries, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Finger injuries presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Finger injuries with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Finger injuries is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Finger injuries should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Finger injuries is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Finger injuries, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Finger injuries may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Finger injuries patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Finger injuries, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Finger injuries in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Finger injuries, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Finger injuries does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Finger injuries may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Finger injuries, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Finger injuries as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Finger injuries draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Hand and Wrist Injuries

_Source lines: 34635-34961._

```text
II. HAND AND WRIST INJURIES
Injuries and infections of the wrist and hand result in over 6 million visits to the ED
each year.

A. Descriptive terms
1. Location. The terms radial, ulnar, palmar (volar), and dorsal are used to de-
scribe the location of the hand injury.

2. Digits. The digits can be numbered or named: I (thumb), II (index finger),
III (long finger), IV (ring finger), and V (little finger).

3. Joints. The joints are the distal interphalangeal (DIP), proximal interphalan-
geal (PIP), metacarpophalangeal (MCP), and carpometacarpal.

B. Diagnosis
1. Patient history. Important aspects of the history include hand dominance
(right-handed versus left-handed), details of the mechanism of injury, the posi-
tion of the hand at the time of injury (e.g., fist, fingers extended), the timing
and nature of all symptoms, the patient's occupation, previous hand deficits,
and the patient's medical history.

2. Physical examination
a. Inspection. Abnormal flexion or extension of individual digits when the
hand is at rest should be noted, as should swelling, scars, amputations, and
discolorations.

b. Palpation of the entire hand and wrist should be performed to determine
deformity or point tenderness (see Clinical Pearl 18-2).

c. Tendon function assessment. Each muscle-tendon group is tested individu-
ally by determining strength and pain against resistance as well as pain with
active and passive motion. When the tendon is completely ruptured, there
is no movement; with partial ruptures, intact motion may exist but pain and
decreased strength are present.

i. The deep flexor tendons are assessed by having the patient flex each
finger at the DIP joint while the examiner keeps the PIP and MCP
joints of all the patient's fingers in extension.

ii. The superficial flexor tendons are assessed in a similar fashion by
having the patient flex each PIP joint while the examiner keeps the
patient's other fingers in extension.

iii. The individual extensor tendons are assessed by having the patient
extend all digits. The thumb should be extended and abducted (away
from the palm) against resistance.

d. Wrist function assessment. Wrist function is examined by having the
patient flex the wrist, extend the wrist, and deviate the wrist to the radial
and ulnar side against resistance.

e. Neurologic examination
i. Motor function
a) Because the median nerve innervates the abductor pollicis brevis,
this nerve can be tested by having the patient abduct the thumb
against resistance.

b) The ulnar nerve innervates the hypothenar muscles, the thumb
adductor, and the intrinsic interosseous muscles of the hand. This
nerve can be tested by determining the strength of the index and
little fingers when the patient is instructed to abduct the fingers
against resistance.

Quick 	HIT
When inspecting a tendon
for injury through an open
wound, the position of the
hand and fingers must be
the same as they were at the
time of injury.

Navicular Fracture
The navicular bone lies in the anatomist's snuff-box; palpation for tenderness is necessary to detect fractures
of the navicular bone, which are often occult and not visible radiographically. This is a commonly missed frac-
ture and evaluation for this injury should be documented.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	18-2
O R T H O P E D I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	425

Orthopedic Emergencies
c) The radial nerve is injured if the patient cannot extend the wrist
against resistance (wristdrop).

ii. Sensation
a) Both the radial and ulnar aspect of each digit (digital nerves) should
be tested using 5-mm two-point touch.

b) All three nerves to the hand should be assessed by determining
sensation at the dorsal web space between the thumb and index
finger (radial nerve), the tip of the long finger (median nerve), and
the tip of the little finger (ulnar nerve). Light touch should be used
to test sensation in the dorsal aspect of the fingers and hand because
this area is less sensitive than the palmar aspect.

f. Arterial blood flow assessment. The color, warmth, and nail bed capillary
refill time should be examined in each finger. Both radial and ulnar pulses
should be palpated.

C. Treatment
1. Wounds that penetrate the skin frequently damage tendons, nerves, vessels,
and joint capsules. Deep sutures should never be placed in the hand by ED
personnel (except to repair extensor tendons). Elevation, splinting, and close
follow-up are important components in the care of hand wounds.

2. Tendon injuries. Although some complete extensor tendon ruptures can be
repaired in the ED, flexor tendon injuries should never be repaired in the ED
and always require orthopedic consultation.

D. Specific injuries

## 1. Hand

a. Infections
i. Paronychia is the most common hand infection and presents as
swelling, erythema, pain, and tenderness around the base of the nail.

The causative organism is usually Staphylococcus or Streptococcus.

Following appropriate anesthesia, drainage is performed by inserting
a scalpel blade between the nail and the nail fold. Antibiotics are
controversial.

ii. Felon is an extremely painful infection of the pulp tissue in the tip
of a digit. The causative organism is usually Staphylococcus, and the
major symptom is pain and swelling of the fingertip. Felons should
be drained by making an incision along one lateral aspect of the
fingertip then separating the septum of the fingertip by blunt dis-
section; close follow-up is necessary and antibiotics are commonly
prescribed.

iii. Tenosynovitis usually occurs along a flexor tendon and is characterized
by tenderness over the tendon, swelling of one finger, pain on passive
extension, and a flexed resting position of the finger. If detected early,
a trail of high-dose intravenous antibiotics is indicated, but frequently,
surgical drainage is required.

iv. Cellulitis of the hand typically requires hospital admission, appropriate
intravenous antibiotics immobilization, and elevation.

v. Septic arthritis of any digital joint or of the carpometacarpal joint
can occur. Treatment entails irrigation and drainage in the operating
room.

b. Tendon injuries
i. Mallet finger is caused by detachment of the extensor tendon from the
distal phalanx. The patient usually complains of pain at the DIP joint
and is unable to fully extend the affected finger. The radiograph of the
finger may appear normal, or there may be a small avulsion fracture at
the base of the distal phalanx. Unless accompanied by a fracture, mallet
finger is treated with a dorsal splint that prevents flexion of the PIP for
6 weeks. If it is accompanied by a large avulsion fracture, surgical pin-
ning may be required.

Quick 	HIT
The radial nerve innervates
no muscles in the hand.

Quick 	HIT
Partial tendon tears can
be treated by splinting
the finger (possibly for as
long as 6 weeks) and close
follow-up.

426 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Orthopedic Emergencies
ii. Boutonniere deformity, a disruption of the extensor hood apparatus
near the PIP, can be easily overlooked. In the ED, it is treated with a
dorsal splint with the PIP in full extension or slight flexion. This injury
requires close follow-up.

c. Trauma
i. Fingertip amputations are common injuries that require copious
irrigation and careful debridement in the ED. If the distal phalanx is
not exposed, the digit can be dressed and the patient referred to a hand
surgeon for definitive care within a few days.

ii. Finger sprains
a) Collateral ligament sprains of the MCP, PIP, and DIP are common.

Lateral stress should be applied to the extended joint to deter-
mine the amount of laxity and the degree of sprain. All sprains
should be splinted for several weeks or until the follow-up
examination.

b) Third-degree sprains that cannot be reduced in the ED frequently
require surgical repair. "Gamekeeper's thumb" ("skier's thumb") is
a serious injury that is caused by disruption of the ulnar collateral
ligament of the MCP joint of the thumb. These injuries require
splinting and close follow-up.

c) The most significant carpometacarpal joint sprain involves the
first metacarpal joint. These sprains are caused by hyperextension,
and the joint may be dislocated. Severe sprains require operative
treatment.

iii. Finger dislocations. Dislocations of the DIP and PIP joints are usu-
ally easily reduced using longitudinal traction following digital block
anesthesia. Reductions of MCP dislocations can be extremely difficult
and may require surgery. All reduced dislocations should be splinted for
several weeks or until the follow-up examination.

iv. Finger fractures
a) Phalanx fractures
1) Distal phalanx fractures are usually treated with a protective
splint, leaving all joints free. Subungual hematomas are common
and should be drained to alleviate pain. Associated nail bed inju-
ries should be repaired. As discussed in II.D.1.b.i, some fractures
associated with mallet finger require surgery.

2) Middle and proximal phalanx fractures that are not displaced
can be splinted and the patient referred a hand specialist or
orthopedist. Fractures that are displaced may require surgical
pinning after reduction.

b) Metacarpal fractures usually occur at the distal neck rather than at
the head, shaft, or proximal base. All metacarpal fractures can be
treated with an ulnar gutter splint in the ED prior to referral.

1) Distal neck fractures. The most common distal neck fracture is
"boxer's fracture" (i.e., fracture of the neck of the fifth metacarpal).

Distal fractures of the fourth or fifth metacarpal require reduction
if the angulation is greater than 30 degrees.

2) Head, shaft, or base fractures require early follow-up.

v. High-pressure injection injuries are discussed in Chapter 19.V.E.

2. Wrist injuries
a. Carpal dislocations (Figure 18-4)
i. Anterior (volar) lunate dislocation is the most common carpal
dislocation. The lunate appears rotated on the lateral radiograph.

ii. Posterior perilunate dislocation is also common. The capitate is
dorsally dislocated in relation to the lunate.

b. Carpal fractures may occur when a patient falls on an outstretched hand.

Because carpal fractures can be radiographically occult, any patient with
Quick 	HIT
The patient is unable to
grasp items between the
thumb and index finger.

Quick 	HIT
Distal fractures of the
second or third metacarpal
require reduction if the
angulation is greater than
15 degrees.

Quick 	HIT
Initially, there may be only
mild swelling and tingling,
but high-pressure injuries
(despite their initial near-
normal appearance) require
immediate consultation by a
hand surgeon.

Quick 	HIT
Lunate is the most common
carpal dislocation.

O R T H O P E D I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	427

Orthopedic Emergencies
significant signs or symptoms of a wrist fracture should be treated with a
thumb spica splint and referred to an orthopedic surgeon.

i. The scaphoid (navicular), triquetrum, and lunate are the most
commonly fractured carpal bones (Figure 18-5).

ii. Distal radius fractures with significant displacement are usually reduced
in the ED.

a) Colles fracture is a fracture of the distal radius in which the distal
radius fracture fragment is dorsally displaced, producing a character-
istic "dinner fork" deformity.

b) Smith fracture is a fracture of the distal radius characterized by volar dis-
placement (i.e., it is a reversed Colles fracture) of the fracture fragment.

c. Carpal tunnel syndrome is caused by inflammation of the carpal canal,
resulting in compression of the median nerve.

i. Etiology. Carpal tunnel syndrome is usually a result of repetitive wrist
flexion.

ii. Clinical signs. Patients usually complain of paresthesia ("pins and needles")
in the index finger, long finger, and radial aspect of the ring finger.

iii. Treatment. Emergency treatment consists of a volar wrist splint,
nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and referral to a hand specialist.

Quick 	HIT
The paresthesia is exacer-
bated by tapping the volar
wrist (Tinel sign) or by hold-
ing the wrist in the flexed
position (Phalen sign).

A 	B
F I G U R E
18-4 	Lunate and perilunate dislocation.

F I G U R E
18-5 	Scaphoid fracture.

(Courtesy of Colleen Campbell, MD; from Greenberg MI, et al.

Greenberg's Text-Atlas of Emergency Medicine. Philadelphia:
Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2005.)

428 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Orthopedic Emergencies
```

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
